description: On the morning of 29 May, under high cloud cover, around 9 am, I found an Empid that caught my eye. Structurally and behaviorally, it made me think Willow Flycatcher. However, its back was greener than any Willow I've seen, the wings showed a great deal of contrast between coverts/tertials and their edges, and the bird had an eyering, albeit a very narrow one. Alder Flycatcher came to mind, and I checked on other ID features (see below). After about 30 seconds, the bird was nice enough to start calling.   This was a moderately active Empid, seemingly larger than the Leasts I'd seen earlier (by impression anyway, including tail, which seemed longer and wider). The back was moderately bright green, brighter than that of any Willow/Dusky/Hammond I'd seen this spring. Some Leasts were probably about as bright. However, there was not the distinct contrast between back and nape that Leasts show. I did not carefully scrutinize color of nape and crown, but they were at least somewhat green, not with prominent gray or brown hues. The wing was quite blackish, like a Least. The wingbars were very contrasting white, as were the tertial edges, like a bright Least. The primary extension was long, longer than any Willow or Dusky I'd seen but probably not as long as that of a Hammond's.   The belly was washed lightly in yellow. The throat appeared quite white. The chest color was not specifically noted, but was dingy. The face was greenish with a narrow, circular, complete white eyering, distinctly narrower than the eyering of Hammond's/Least/Dusky for comparison. The bill was largish, but seemed a tad narrower than that of a Willow (perhaps because I was expecting such... I must admit). The lower mandible was completely pink.Behaviors: This was a moderately active EmpidCall: Call: given about 8 times, about 5 seconds apart. Call something between a "Peek" and a "Jeek." Not quite like a Hammond's Fly, which I think of as sounding rather like a LB Dowitcher.Plumage: Adult
